data.description:
Sibling of f6f6-bc8e (fsck recover under readonly), found auditing for that antipattern.

## Antipattern
A mutating capability is reachable from the MCP read/ungated section. f6f6 gated recover=True, but plain fsck() ALSO mutates: ticket-fsck.sh:180 `rm -f "$lock_file"` removes a stale (>5min) .git/index.lock. The script header says "Non-destructive except for stale index.lock removal" — i.e. it IS destructive. mcp_server.py fsck() only gates recover, so plain fsck() runs the rm under REBAR_MCP_READONLY=1.

## Experiment (confirmed)
Built a REBAR_MCP_READONLY=1 server (build_server) on a real rebar repo, created a stale index.lock (mtime -10min), called the fsck tool with recover=False: returned "FIXED: removed stale .git/index.lock (older than 5 minutes)" and the lock was DELETED. A read-only server performed a git-state write.

## Related (flagged, not yet experimentally run — needs acli)
reconcile(mode=dry-run) is cap-0 so allowed under readonly, but per 9d7c it writes bridge_state/ AND files invariant bug tickets (reconcile.py:672 non-filtered passes file bugs = ticket CREATEs). That is a store write reachable under a readonly server. Verify + gate.

## Fix direction
Either make plain fsck non-mutating under readonly (skip the lock rm, just report it), or gate the lock-removal behind a writable server. Audit all read-section tools for hidden writes (reconcile dry-run, validate, bridge_fsck).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Under REBAR_MCP_READONLY=1, fsck() does not remove index.lock (reports only)
- [ ] reconcile dry-run under readonly does not write the store / file tickets (verify + gate)
- [ ] Audit + test matrix for write side-effects of every read-section MCP tool
data.title: MCP fsck() (plain, recover=False) removes stale .git/index.lock under REBAR_MCP_READONLY — mutation on a read-only server
